one_line_takeaway: Translating jailbreak prompts into low-resource African languages alone barely works anymore, but combining low-resource-language translation with multi-turn conversational jailbreaking and (especially) human-refined translations still bypasses commercial LLM guardrails 60-90% of the time, and the paper's correlation analysis (r=0.87-0.92) indicates the remaining resistance in isiXhosa/isiZulu is a translation-quality artifact, not a genuine safety-training advantage for those languages.
key_finding: Single-turn translation-only attacks on the MultiJail dataset were largely ineffective on current models: only DeepSeek exceeded a 10% harmful-response rate (17.4% isiZulu, 13.0% Kiswahili), and language choice did not significantly predict harmful vs. non-harmful output (chi2=8.07, p=0.089, df=4), contradicting Deng et al.'s 2023 finding of an 83.49% Kiswahili harmful-response rate on GPT-3.5-turbo-0613. Multi-turn attacks were far more effective: English baseline harmful-response rates ranged 52.7% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 83.6% (GPT-4o-mini); Afrikaans 60.0%-78.2%; Kiswahili 41.8% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 70.9% (DeepSeek); isiXhosa 21.8% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 58.2% (Grok-3-mini); isiZulu 21.8% (Gemini-2.0-flash-lite) to 58.2% (GPT-4o-mini). Language had a significant effect on harmful-response probability in the multi-turn setting (chi2=92.58, p<0.001, df=4) for all models except Gemini-2.0-flash-lite. Translation quality correlated strongly with jailbreak success (Pearson r=0.92 for BLEU, 0.91 METEOR, 0.87 BERTScore), supporting the paper's claim that isiXhosa/isiZulu's lower automated success reflects poor translation rather than stronger guardrails. Human red-teaming substantially outperformed automated translation overall (75.82% vs. 59.84% mean harmful-response rate, chi2=37.412, p<0.001), with the largest gains for Afrikaans (+20.04%, to 89.39%) and moderate gains for isiZulu (+12.65%) and isiXhosa (+12.31%); Kiswahili barely changed (+0.99%, single translator only). Claude 3.5 Haiku showed the lowest harmful-response rates across every language tested, indicating the most robust guardrails among the five models; DeepSeek-V3 and GPT-4o-mini were the most vulnerable.
core_contribution: The paper (Marx & Dunaiski, Stellenbosch University) empirically tests whether multi-turn conversational jailbreaks conducted in four low-resource African languages (Afrikaans, Kiswahili, isiXhosa, isiZulu) bypass safety guardrails in five commercial LLMs (ChatGPT/GPT-4o(-mini), Claude 3.5 Haiku, DeepSeek-V3, Gemini-2.0-flash(-lite), Grok-3-mini), comparing automated machine-translated prompts against human red-teaming by native speakers. The paper's own contribution is: (1) a translated dataset built from the existing MultiJail (314 single-turn prompts) and MHJ (537 multi-turn conversations) datasets, filtered via a novel per-language quality threshold (tau = mean + std dev across BERTScore/METEOR/BLEU on back-translated text) that retained only 47 high-quality MultiJail and 55 high-quality MHJ translations; (2) automated single-turn and multi-turn evaluation across the five commercial APIs; (3) a human red-teaming arm where native speakers refined the machine translations and adapted conversation strategy across up to 10 turns against GPT-4o, Gemini-2-flash, and Grok; and (4) a correlation analysis linking translation-quality metrics to jailbreak success. Its central finding is that single-turn translation-only jailbreaks are now largely ineffective, but multi-turn conversational jailbreaks remain highly effective across languages, and that translation quality -- not stronger per-language safety training -- is the dominant factor limiting jailbreak success in isiXhosa and isiZulu.
threat_model: Black-box, API-only adversary with no model-weight access, targeting commercial LLM chat APIs (ChatGPT/GPT-4o family, Claude 3.5 Haiku, DeepSeek-V3, Gemini-2.0 family, Grok-3-mini) via crafted natural-language prompts alone. Two attack surfaces are tested: (a) single-turn attacks that translate an already-harmful English prompt into a low-resource language and submit it directly; (b) multi-turn attacks that distribute harmful intent across a sequence of individually-innocuous-seeming prompts (using the MHJ dataset's hidden-intention-streamlining conversations), executed either fully automatically via Google Translate or via a human-in-the-loop red-teamer who adapts the conversation based on model responses. The presumed vulnerability class is a mismatch between English-centric safety training/guardrails and model behavior in low-resource languages, exploited jointly with the known multi-turn/context-distribution jailbreak vector.
limitations: Paper's own stated limitations: only four low-resource languages were tested, chosen by resource level and translator availability, so findings may not generalize to other low-resource languages; the strict quality threshold discarded many machine-translated conversations, shrinking usable sample sizes (down to 47 and 55 high-quality prompts); automated evaluation relies on back-translation to English for quality scoring, which can itself distort semantic content and bias the quality metric; human translator time constraints capped conversations per translator (10 turns max, and only 1 Kiswahili translator vs. 3 for other languages, limiting statistical power for that language); and the target models are continually updated, so results may not replicate later. Additional grounding-relevant caveats visible in the results: sample sizes per language/model cell in the human red-teaming breakdown (Table 6) are small (N=9-26), and single-turn vs. multi-turn datasets differ in origin (MultiJail vs. MHJ), so the single-turn/multi-turn comparison is not a controlled ablation on identical prompts.

datasets_used: MultiJail (Deng et al., 2024) - 314 single-turn harmful English prompts, translated into Afrikaans/Kiswahili/isiZulu/isiXhosa
MHJ - Multi-turn Human Jailbreak dataset (Li et al., 2024a) - 537 successful multi-turn jailbreak conversations
Authors' own filtered subset: 47 high-quality translated MultiJail prompts and 55 high-quality translated MHJ conversations, retained after applying the tau=mean+SD BERTScore/METEOR/BLEU threshold
quantitative_results: Multi-turn English baseline harmful-response rate: 52.7% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 83.6% (GPT-4o-mini)
Multi-turn Afrikaans harmful-response rate: 60.0% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 78.2% (GPT-4o-mini/DeepSeek-V3)
Multi-turn Kiswahili harmful-response rate: 41.8% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 70.9% (DeepSeek)
Multi-turn isiXhosa harmful-response rate: 21.8% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 58.2% (Grok-3-mini)
Single-turn harmful-response rate (MultiJail): only DeepSeek exceeded 10%, at 17.4% (isiZulu) and 13.0% (Kiswahili); all other model/language cells below 10%
Language x response-type chi-square (single-turn): chi2=61.75, p<0.001, df=8; harmful-vs-nonharmful only: chi2=8.07, p=0.089, df=4 (not significant)
Language x response-type chi-square (multi-turn): chi2=147.89, p<0.001, df=8; harmful-vs-nonharmful: chi2=92.58, p<0.001, df=4 (significant)
Pearson correlation between translation quality and jailbreak success: BLEU r=0.92, METEOR r=0.91, BERTScore r=0.87
Human red-teaming vs. automated overall mean harmful-response rate: 75.82% vs. 59.84% (chi2=37.412, p<0.001)

Human red-teaming gains by language (Table 4): English +15.32% (72.73% to 88.05%), Afrikaans +20.04% (69.35% to 89.39%), isiXhosa +12.31% (45.71% to 58.02%), isiZulu +12.65% (49.09% to 61.74%), Kiswahili +0.99% (62.34% to 63.33%)
Translation quality metrics, mean scores (Table 1): Afrikaans BERTScore 0.94/METEOR 0.88/BLEU 0.67; Kiswahili 0.91/0.79/0.52; isiZulu 0.90/0.78/0.50; isiXhosa 0.89/0.76/0.48
Individual human translator variation: Afrikaans 86.67%-91.03%; isiXhosa 36.67%-74.07%; isiZulu 29.17%-78.79%; English evaluators 79.49%-93.75%
Common Crawl resource shares used to classify languages as low-resource: Afrikaans 0.0095%, Kiswahili 0.0095%, isiZulu 0.0011%, isiXhosa 0.0007% (all below the 0.1% low-resource cutoff)
baselines_compared: English-language prompts (both single-turn and multi-turn) used as the within-study baseline against which each low-resource language's harmful-response rate is compared
Automated (Google Translate) prompt pipeline compared against human-refined red-teaming translations

Cited external baseline (not reproduced experimentally, referenced only): Deng et al. (2024) reported an 83.49% Kiswahili harmful-response rate on GPT-3.5-turbo-0613 in single-turn MultiJail testing, which this paper's single-turn results (all under ~17%) contradict for current-generation models
Five commercial LLMs compared against each other as attack targets: GPT-4o/GPT-4o-mini, Claude-3.5-Haiku, DeepSeek-V3, Gemini-2.0-flash/flash-lite, Grok-3-mini
